messages.content: You adjudicate whether found evidence supports, contradicts, or is silent on a market-risk hypothesis. Every stance needs a VERBATIM quote from the result text. Status: 'corroborated' = ≥1 independent source materially supports the mechanism's key premise; 'refuted' = a LOAD-BEARING premise is directly contradicted by a strong source; 'contested' = credible contradiction of a non-load-bearing link; 'speculative' = no signal either way (absence of coverage is NOT refutation). You may revise the likelihood in a bounded way with a rationale; you may NOT change the perturbation or target nodes.

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "title": "The Netherlands is CE's hyperscale self-build hotspot — its 28% geography weight counts MW that carry the least PDU content in the region",
 "narrative": "The geo split apportions tamBase by colo MW as if every megawatt carries equal PDU spend. But CBRE places ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — and NL is the only CE-7 country on that list. Self-build halls are precisely where busway + OCP power-shelf architectures are set as default, and Amsterdam (70% of NL colo) is where those same hyperscale tenants lease wholesale suites. So the NL slice is disproportionately composed of substitution-leading capacity, while Germany's mix skews toward colo/enterprise halls that still buy PDUs. The model's 0.28 NL weight silently assumes PDU intensity is geography-neutral; in CE it is not, and the error concentrates in the second-largest geography node.",
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "CBRE: ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity is located in Ireland, Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium (Q4 2025) — NL is the sole CE-7 member, and European self-build is growing 24% YoY, faster than colocation's 19%",

   ]
  },

   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "Self-build hyperscale halls are the architecture-setting sites for busway/shelf distribution; combined with Amsterdam's ~70% concentration of NL colo capacity absorbed heavily by hyperscale tenants, NL MW carry systematically lower discrete-PDU content per MW than DE or CH MW",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:geo.NL",
    "digest:seg-mix#c24"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "geo.NL's 0.28 is derived purely from MW apportionment (951/3,451) with a band (0.24–0.31) that only reflects capacity-count uncertainty, not PDU-intensity variation; the PDU-euro share of NL is below its capacity share, overstating the NL slice of tamBase in absolute terms",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:geo.NL",
    "ledger:tamBase"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "falsifier": "Vendor sell-through or fit-out BOM data showing NL rack-PDU revenue per colo MW in line with the CE average (e.g., a distributor split or Omdia country table showing NL PDU intensity ≈ DE's).",

  "rationale": "The self-build concentration in NL is directly sourced (CBRE) and the low-PDU-content nature of self-build halls is well documented for AI-density builds; offsetting this, Amsterdam retains a large retail-colo and enterprise base, and Dutch grid constraints slow new self-build, limiting how fast the mix shifts within 12 months.",

 }

Data centre capacity owned and operated exclusively by hyperscalers, also known as self-builds, in Europe is expected to outpace the growth of colocation supply in 2026, according to new research from CBRE.

The latest research shows that hyperscaler self-build capacity across Europe is expected to reach 4.2GW this year, representing 24% year-on-year growth compared to 2025. This new supply will be delivered across nine European countries, marking the seventeenth consecutive year of double-digit expansion for the segment.

Hyperscalers are set to deliver a record level of self-build capacity this year as they expand cloud regions and support increasing volumes of equipment dedicated to artificial intelligence workloads. As of Q4 2025, approximately 60% of Europe’s operational hyperscaler self-build capacity is located in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium.

# CBRE: European hyperscaler self-build capacity growth to outpace colocation supply growth, but supply outpaces take-up in weaker than expected 2025

Hyperscaler self-build to increase by 24 percent in 2026

February 20, 2026 By Barney Dixon Have your say

Growth of European data center capacity operated and owned exclusively by hyperscalers will outpace colocation supply growth in the region this year, according to research from real estate services firm CBRE.

CBRE’s Europe Data Centres report, which covers Q4 2025, predicts hyperscaler self-build capacity will reach 4.2GW in 2026, a 24 percent increase on the previous year and the seventeenth consecutive year of double-digit growth.

In comparison, CBRE expects the top 15 European colocation data center markets to grow 19 percent year-on-year. The colocation segment is expected to remain about 50 percent larger than the self-build segment at the end of 2026.

– Getty Images

This new supply will be delivered across nine European countries, with 60 percent of Europe’s operational hyperscale self-build capacity located in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden, and Belgium.

### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Demand for capacity in Europe is high, though a lack of available power across metro markets is expected to inhibit growth to varying degrees. Some organisations are likely to look for capacity in new areas where supply can be more easily sourced, and the cost of power is lower.

### Key Takeaways

1. Strong demand from hyperscalers is expected to push data centre take-up to new highs in 2024. Vacancy rates are likely to fall to record lows across Europe’s largest markets.
2. Colocation data centre providers will likely hike rental rates further, given the lack of supply as well as the rising development and operational costs. Given an expected decline in available space, organisations are likely to seek capacity in facilities that are further from existing well-developed data centre markets.
3. A lack of available power in European metro markets is expected to inhibit the growth of data centre providers looking to meet the needs of the hyperscalers who are their largest customers.

## Appetite for data centre space expected to remain strong despite higher rates

# Pure DC secures hyperscale customer for 78MW data center campus in Netherlands

Work on first phase to start next month

December 16, 2025 By Dan Swinhoe Have your say

Pure Data Centres Group (Pure DC) has secured a tenant for a planned campus in the Netherlands.

The company this week announced it has signed “2025’s largest standalone hyperscale data center lease in Europe,” taking all available capacity at a planned site in Amsterdam.

– Pure DC

An unnamed hyperscale customer is leasing the entire 78MW campus, situated in Westpoort, Amsterdam, with Pure DC investing more than €1 billion ($877 million) to develop the 5.6-acre site.

As part of the lease deal, Pure DC has purchased a site, secured planning permissions, and 100MVA of power via a private substation. The land was purchased on a long leasehold from the Port of Amsterdam.

Pure’s AMS01 campus will comprise three 26MW buildings and a private substation with a connection into the 50kV grid. The three multi-story buildings will total 94,660 sqm (1 million sq ft).

## AMS1 CyrusOne Data Center

Linieweg 1,1165 AA, Halfweg, Netherlands

#### Introduction

CyrusOne AMS1 is a brand new purpose built state-of-the art data center campus offering cloud providers, systems integrators, and multinational corporations customized, secure, and resilient data center solutions within a key business hub.

Strategically located in Amsterdam, one of Europe’s premier data center locations, the facility will ultimately deliver 29,350 sqm (315,920 sq ft) of world-class technical space with an IT load of 54 MW, making it the largest build-to-suit campus in the country.

#### Overview

• 29,350 sqm (315,920 sq ft) of world-class technical space across a campus of two separate buildings 
• 54 MW of total IT load to the facility, 27 MW to each of the two separate buildings 
• Strategically located along longhaul fibre routes for multiple carriers. Landing stations connect the site to the UK, Denmark, France and the USA, offering a connectivity gateway to the Amsterdam metro network with a peak traffic flow through the Amsterdam Internet Exchange (AMS-IX) 
• Highly resilient, concurrently maintainable power and cooling to Tier III 
• Dedicated electrical plant to each floor 
• Low PUE through the use of free cooling chillers 
• 900 mm heavy duty raised floor with 3,600 mm clear height in the data hall 
• Active / Active dual redundant power supplies of 60 MW 
• Carrier neutrality and diverse fibre connectivity from multiple providers

# Pure Data Centres launches new Amsterdam data centre fully leased with power and permits

## Supporting digital growth, energy resilience and skilled job creation in one of Europe’s most constrained markets for digital infrastructure

Topic Company News

Type News, Press releases, Pure DC

Date 15/12/2025

Author Pure DC

Read Time 4 Minutes

#### Pure Data Centres Group (Pure DC), the designer, developer, and operator of hyperscale cloud and AI data centres, today announced the launch of its new Amsterdam data centre. At launch, the campus is already fully leased with power and permits in place. Situated in Westpoort, Amsterdam, Pure DC will be investing over €1 billion to develop the site.

As part of the deal, Pure DC has purchased a site, secured planning permissions, and 100MVA of power via a private substation. The land was purchased on a long leasehold from the Port of Amsterdam. Securing, the site, combined with permitting approvals, power, and supply constraints within Amsterdam required complex negotiations and creative partnership over many months to secure the deal.

This major infrastructure investment is set to play a pivotal role in supporting the region’s digital growth and energy resilience. ## Global Switch Amsterdam East (Amsterdam, The Netherlands)

OCP Ready™ Data Center, Global Switch Amsterdam: Average density of 3,000W/m2, accommodating up to 50kW racks and supporting transition to liquid cooling.

Global Switch Amsterdam is located in the Slotervaart/Overtoomseveld district, adjacent to the main Dutch cable infrastructure. This world-class data center campus is just 2km from the financial district and city center.

- The technical suites will be cooled using fan wall units, configured as a flooded room with hot aisle containment and supported from a free-cooling water-based system.
- Amsterdam East will lead the way in an environmentally sustainable design, targeting a BREEAM Very Good rating and annualised PUE of less than 1.2. 100% of the electricity purchased, including the IT power, will be sourced from renewables. Standby generation will use biofuel and refrigerant gas will have low GWP and zero ODP.
- Each suite has the capacity of 2MW at an average density of 3,000W/m2 and contiguous space of 4MW can be provided. Up to 50kW racks can be currently accomodated and the suites can be transitioned for liquid cooling to support higher rack power requirements.
- Access to peering platforms AMS-IX and NL-IX for direct access to the global IP backbone.
- Customers will benefit from direct access to the already network-dense, low latency ecosyst"

Amsterdam's hyperscale self-build/wholesale lease activity (Pure DC 78MW fully leased) and OCP-ready architecture in NL colo halls corroborate the propagation mechanism qualitatively. This modestly strengthens the hypothesis. But the load-bearing quantitative claim (NL PDU-euro/MW below CE average) remains unverified, and the Amsterdam moratorium redirecting build to other Dutch metros partially undercuts the '70% Amsterdam' sub-premise — keeping the revision small and near the original estimate."}
